text: Procedure and Results 6-7
Description of the System Simulated: A schematic flow sheet of the MSRE system is shown in Figure 1.
The temperature symbols are identified in Figure 2.
The design information used in these studies is listed in Figure 3.
The analog computer diagram is filed in the Engineering and Mechanical Division print files on Drawings 40331 and 40332.

The "time constant" of the controller was assumed to be 50 milliseconds.
This "time constant" is the time required for the rod speed to attain $63\%$ of full speed subsequent to receiving a demand signal to move the rods.
The "dead band" of the controller was $\pm 2^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ .

The first term in $\epsilon_{1}$ can be considered as a high frequency band pass stabilizing mechanism.

\frac {1}{G} \epsilon_ {4} = m \left\{\mathrm {a} \phi - \left(\mathrm {T} _ {\underline {{i}}} \right] _ {\mathrm {s}} - \left. \mathrm {T} _ {\circ} \right] _ {\mathrm {s}}) - g (\mathrm {t}) \right\} + n \left(\mathrm {T} _ {\circ} \right] _ {\mathrm {r}} - \left. \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {s p}}\right)
This control signal is the same as $\epsilon_{3}$ except that a different controlled variable is used.
The analog computer diagram used to derive $\epsilon_{4}$ is shown in Figure 7.
Using $\mathbf{E}_{\mathrm{i}}$ as the control signal, M, as shown on the computer diagram, was set at .877 (quite arbitrarily) and N was set at 0.5.
The load, or the heat removal rate by the air across the radiator, was set at approximately $\frac{1}{2}$ megawatt and the system permitted to stabilize.
The load was increased from $\frac{1}{2}$ mw to 10 mw in 15 seconds, approximately at a constant rate.
The curves obtained are shown in Figure 8.
Using $\epsilon_{2}$ as the control signal, M, as shown on the computer diagram, was set at 0.877 and N set at 0.5.
The load was changed from approximately 1.6 mw to 10 mw in 17 seconds.
The resulting curves are shown in Figure 9.
Using $\mathbf{E}_3$ as the control signal, M, as shown on the computer diagram, was set at 0.25 and N was set at 0.5.
The load was changed from approximately 1.6 mw to 10 mw in 13 seconds.
The resulting curves are shown in Figure 10.
Using $\mathbf{E}_{\psi}$ as the control signal, M, as shown on the computer diagram, was set at 0.50 and N was set at 1.00.
The load was changed from approximately 1.6 mw to 10 mw in ll seconds.
